title: Mathematical Basis for Analyzing Superconducting Phase Transitions Using Catastrophe Theory
authors: Jiu Hui Wu, Hua Tian, Kejiang Zhou
abstract: We establish a rigorous mathematical bridge from quantum many-body path integrals to the cusp catastrophe model by Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, which provides a theoretical foundation for analyzing superconducting phase transition using the catastrophe theory. First, it is proved that, near the critical point the infinite-dimensional effective action is diffeomorphic to a finite-dimensional catastrophe. Secondly, starting from Ginzburg-Landau free energy functional, the Euler-Lagrange partial differential equation can be reduced to the cusp catastrophe model. Thirdly, the fermionic imaginary-time path integral to the cusp catastrophe is derived through the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, Matsubara frequency expansion, and Grassmann algebra. Furthermore, we connect this framework with the adsorption potential theory we proposed, elucidating the catastrophic topological nature of the electron pairing mechanism in high-temperature superconductivity. The precise microscopic derivation of the adsorption potential from first-principles electronic structure calculations would strengthen the predictive power of the theory.
summary: This paper develops a novel mathematical bridge connecting the complex quantum many-body physics of superconductors to the well-established framework of Catastrophe Theory. By using techniques like Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction on path integrals, the authors show that superconducting phase transitions must mathematically reduce to the universal cusp catastrophe model near criticality. This provides a powerful, topologically grounded understanding of pairing mechanisms in high-temperature superconductivity.
main_problem: To establish a rigorous mathematical framework connecting quantum many-body path integrals to Catastrophe Theory for analyzing superconducting phase transitions.
main_result: The analysis proves that near the critical point, the infinite-dimensional effective action governing superconductivity is mathematically equivalent (diffeomorphic) to a finite-dimensional cusp catastrophe model.
method: The authors employ Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction on the Euler-Lagrange equations derived from Ginzburg-Landau theory and fermionic path integrals. They utilize advanced mathematical tools like Hubbard-Stratonovich transformations and Grassmann algebra to achieve this reduction.
model_or_system: The study focuses on superconducting systems, particularly high-temperature superconductors, modeled initially by the Ginzburg-Landau free energy functional ($\Gamma$) and extended via fermionic imaginary-time path integrals. The theoretical machinery bridges quantum field theory concepts to classical singularity theory.
key_observables: Superconducting gap ($\Delta$), effective action ($\mathcal{S}_{	ext{eff}}$), specific heat capacity, and the adsorption potential.
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$) relative to critical temperature ($T_c$); control parameters governing the catastrophe (e.g., $a(T)$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that near criticality, only a finite number of zero-mode directions are 'dangerous,' allowing for reduction to a low-dimensional catastrophe model; it also relies on structural stability theorems.
paper_structure: The paper builds its argument by first showing the equivalence between the infinite-dimensional effective action and a finite-dimensional catastrophe near criticality. It then rigorously derives this reduction starting from the Ginzburg-Landau functional, confirming it maps to the cusp catastrophe model. Finally, it extends this derivation using the full fermionic path integral formalism.
why_interesting: The use of Catastrophe Theory provides a universal, topologically protected description for phase transitions in strongly correlated systems, offering an alternative perspective beyond standard mean-field or BCS approaches that is relevant to understanding critical phenomena across different quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
